content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
May 11,
1995
Larry C. Larsen
Law Offices of
Gregory D.
Thatch
1730 I Street, Suite 220
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—95—1Ol
Dear Mr. Larsen:
This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
Mr.
James Block, member of the Sacramento Private Industry Council
under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act.
1
!
Some of the facts were provided in your written request for
advice as well as in our recent telephone conversations.
QUESTION
Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prevent
Mr.
Block from entering into a contract to supply janitorial
supplies to the Sacramento Employment and Training Agency’s Head
Start Program?
CONCLUSION
Although Mr.
Block’s agency will not be making or
participating in the decision to award this contract,
Mr.
Block is
prohibited from using his official position to attempt to
influence the decision of the Sacramento Employment Training
Agency or the Parent Policy Council.
FACTS
In 1982, the federal government enacted the Job Training
Partnership Act
(“JTPA”) to establish programs to prepare youth
and unskilled adults for entry into the labor force.
The JTPA
1/
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 1 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—95—lO1
Page
2
also provides programs to afford job training to those
economically disadvantaged individuals and other individuals
facing serious barriers to employment,
who are in special need of
such training to obtain productive employment.
The Sacramento Employment and Training Agency
(“sETA”)
administers funds under the JTPA for recipients in the City and
County of Sacramento.
The provisions of the JTPA require the
creation of a Private Industry Council to work in cooperation with
SETA to develop appropriate job training plans.
Each job training
plan is a comprehensive document that contains a two—year program
budget and procedures for identifying and selecting participants,
performance goals,
a description of services to be provided under
the plan,
and procedures for selecting service providers.
The JTPA requires that members of the Private Industry
Council be comprised of representatives of the private sector and
representatives of educational agencies,
organized labor,
rehabilitation agencies,
community—based organizations,
economic
development agencies and the public employment service.
(29
U.S.C.
Section 1512.)
The majority of the Private Industry
Council are members from the private sector.
2
!
In addition to serving as the administrator under the JTPA,
SETA also administers funds under other federal and state
programs,
such as Head Start, the Community Service Block Grant
Act, Greater Avenues for Independence, the Refugee Targeted
Assistance Program and the Stewart B.
McKinney Homeless Act.
Mr.
James Block is a member of the Private Industry Council.
Mr.
Block owns and operates JB Enterprises,
a janitorial supply
company in Sacramento.
He has expressed an interest in competing
for the contract to provide janitorial supplies and equipment to
the Head Start program.
The janitorial supplies and equipment would be utilized not
only for the Head Start office but would be used by school
districts and apartment buildings which are recipients or
participants in the Head Start program.
The Private Industry Council plays no role with respect to
the expenditure of funds under SETA’S Head Start program.
Rather,
the expenditure of Head Start funds is a product of actions taken
by a Parent Policy Council,
comprised largely of parents who have
children enrolled in the Head Start program and the SETA Governing
Board.
Neither Mr.
Block nor any member of the Private Industry
Council,
would be involved in the decision to award this contract
or be involved in decisions concerning this contract.
2/
Government Code Section 1091.2 excludes members of Private
Industry Councils from the prohibitions of Government Code Section
1090, provided that the members comply with the provisions of
Government Code Section 87100.


File No.
A—95—l0l
Page
3
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on .the public generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official’s immediate
family,
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
***
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when, the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
Section 87103 (a) (c) (d)
“Investment” means any financial interest in or security
issued by a business entity,
including but not limited to common
stock, preferred stock, rights, warrants, options, debt
instruments and any partnership or other ownership interest owned
directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official or his
or her immediate family.
(Section 82034.)
“Income” means a payment received,
including but not limited
to any salary, wage,
advance, dividend, interest, rent, proceeds
from any sale, gift,
loan, forgiveness or payment of indebtedness
received by the filer, reimbursement for expenses or per diem.
(Section 82030.)
Mr. Block owns and operates JB Enterprises,
a janitorial
supply company in Sacramento.
Thus, he has an investment interest
in,
and receives income from, that business entity.


File No.
A—95—lOl
Page
4
Accordingly,
Mr.
Block may not make, participate in making,
or use his official position to attempt to influence a
governmental decision if the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable
3
! material financial effect, distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally,
on JB Enterprises.
You indicated that Mr.
Block would like to submit a bid,
in a
competitive bid process,
on a contract to provide janitorial
supplies to SETA’s Head Start program.
Neither Mr.
Block,
nor the
Private Industry Council, for which he is a member, will
participate in the decision to award this contract or be involved
in the development of the contract.
The Head Start Parent Policy
Council and the
SETA Governing Board will make that decision.
Even though neither Mr.
Block,
nor the members of the Private
Industry Council, will be participating in the governmental
decision to award this contract, the Act prohibits Mr.
Block from
using his position to influence the decision of
SETA or the Parent
Policy Council.
Commission Regulation 18700.1(a)
provides that with regard to
a governmental decision which is within or before the official’s
own agency,
or any agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of the official’s agency, the official is attempting to
use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for
the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts,
or
appears before,
or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member,
officer,
employee or consultant of the agency.
(Regulations 18700
and 18700.1.)
This provision would apply to influencing the PlC decisions
or any advisory bodies that are created or have members appointed
by the PlC.
Moreover, Regulation 18700.1(c)
also provides that with
regard to a governmental decision of agencies other than the
official’s own agency,
or agencies appointed by or subject to the
budgetary control of the official’s agency, the official is
attempting to use his official position to influence the decision
if,
for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official acts
or purports to act on behalf of,
or as the representative of, his
or her agency to any member, officer,
employee or consultant of an
agency.
Such actions include, but are not limited to the use of
official stationery.
This provision would apply to influencing
the members of
SETA or members of the Parent Policy Council.
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (
re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)


File No.
A—95—l01
Page
5
Pursuant to Regulation 18700. 1(c),
as long as Mr.
Block does
not act or purport to represent the Private Industry Council, he
is not restricted by the Political Reform Act from entering into a
contract with the Head Start Program or from entering into
communications with the Head Start Program relating to the
contract.
Please note that our advice is limited to the Political
Reform Act.
You may want to seek further guidance from your county
counsel or the Attorney General’s Office regarding other
provisions of the law such as Government Code Section 1090.
I trust this letter has provided you with guidance regarding
the duties and responsibilities of Mr.
Block.
If you have any
further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to
contact the Commission’s Legal Division at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
Gener
1 Cou
el
By.
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prevent
Mr.
Block from entering into a contract to supply janitorial
supplies to the Sacramento Employment and Training Agency’s Head
Start Program?

Although Mr.
Block’s agency will not be making or
participating in the decision to award this contract,
Mr.
Block is
prohibited from using his official position to attempt to
influence the decision of the Sacramento Employment Training
Agency or the Parent Policy Council.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prevent
Mr.
Block from entering into a contract to supply janitorial
supplies to the Sacramento Employment and Training Agency’s Head
Start Program?

CONCLUSION: Although Mr.
Block’s agency will not be making or
participating in the decision to award this contract,
Mr.
Block is
prohibited from using his official position to attempt to
influence the decision of the Sacramento Employment Training
Agency or the Parent Policy Council.
